Researched articles and site copy that respect the reader's time.
Editing and rewrites of existing content

The web does not need more content. It needs fewer, better pieces — researched, sourced, and written to be read rather than to be indexed.

Articles here are reported: interviews conducted, claims checked, sources named. Web copy is built from the same discipline — what does the visitor need to know, in what order, in how few words.

This is the right service for a publication that needs a reliable contributor, or a business whose website says a great deal without saying anything.
Lives recorded properly — from a 400-word obituary to a full memoir.

Everyone has a story that deserves better than a template. This service records lives — sometimes urgently, for an obituary due in days, and sometimes over months of interviews for a memoir.

Memoir work is built on recorded conversation: hours of interviews, transcribed and shaped into a narrative in the subject's own voice. Family members receive both the finished book and the raw recordings — often the more treasured of the two.

Obituaries and eulogies are handled with speed and care. A short call, a draft within a day when needed, and words the family will not wince at later.
Ghostwriting, collaboration, and developmental support from proposal to press.

A book is a project with a thousand ways to stall. This service exists to get manuscripts finished — yours with support, or ghostwritten entirely from your material and voice.

Engagements begin with structure: an outline you approve before serious drafting begins, so the 40,000-word surprises happen on paper, not in month eight. Chapters arrive on a schedule, with your feedback folded into each round.

For authors going the traditional route, that includes proposals and query packages. For those self-publishing, it includes honest guidance about editing, design, and distribution — where to spend and where not to.
Archival, business, and genealogical research — with a source trail you can verify.
Annotated source list and research report
Document retrieval from archives and repositories

Writing fails when research fails. This service exists on its own for clients who need the facts found, verified, and organized — whether or not we do the writing afterward.

Business clients use it for market and competitor background, executive briefings, and due-diligence on claims. Family clients use it to locate records: censuses, vital records, ship manifests, military files, land deeds, newspapers. Authors use it to fact-check a manuscript before it goes to an editor.

Everything delivered comes with citations. If a claim can't be sourced, you'll be told that too — a documented dead end is worth more than a confident guess.
Plain-English explanations of complex products, for the people who buy them.

Most technology writing is written for the people who built the product. Yours should be written for the people who buy it.

The process starts with interviews — your engineers, your customers, your sales calls — and ends with prose a busy decision-maker can read once and understand. No jargon laundering, no filler paragraphs written to hit a word count.

Typical engagements: a white paper series for a B2B software firm, case studies built from customer interviews, or a full website rewrite when the product has outgrown the copy.
Family lines traced, verified, and turned into stories worth keeping.
Documented family trees with source citations
Heirloom books for reunions and anniversaries

A family tree is a diagram. A family history is a story. This service produces both — the verified record, and the narrative that makes it mean something.

Research follows genealogical proof standards: original records where they exist, resolved conflicts where sources disagree, and honest notes where the trail goes cold. The writing then turns those records into people — why they left, what they found, what they carried with them.

The finished product ranges from a documented tree to a bound narrative history, printed for a reunion or an anniversary, that reads like a book because it is one.
Launch, grow, or rescue a newsletter — strategy, editing, and ghostwriting.

A newsletter is a promise made on a schedule. This service helps you make a promise you can keep — and then keep it.

Launch engagements cover positioning (what is this letter, who is it for, why subscribe), cadence, and setup. Ongoing support ranges from editing your drafts to fully ghostwriting issues from your notes and voice.

For existing newsletters that have stalled, a rescue review looks at the archive, the open rates, and the promise itself — and tells you plainly what to change.
Field & Trail Guide to the Roosevelt National Forest
A 128-page published trail guide covering more than 40 trails on the Canyon Lakes Ranger District — edited and carried through to publication for a volunteer organization.
Cover of the Field & Trail Guide, Roosevelt National Forest, Second Edition, published by Poudre Wilderness Volunteers

The *Field & Trail Guide to the Roosevelt National Forest* — subtitled *A Guide to Backcountry Travel on the Canyon Lakes Ranger District* — is a working reference: a 128-page paperback describing more than 40 trails across the Poudre Canyon, Red Feather Lakes, and Estes Park/Big Thompson Canyon areas of northern Colorado, published by Poudre Wilderness Volunteers. The guide proved its usefulness the way field books do: it went back to press for a second edition.

**The work.** Co-edited by John Paul Lumpp and Dave Cantrell, the guide brought together trail descriptions from many contributors into a single consistent, usable book — the editorial craft of turning collective knowledge into a publication that hikers could actually carry and trust.

**The publisher.** Poudre Wilderness Volunteers is the volunteer organization that assists the U.S. Forest Service in patrolling and maintaining the trails of the Canyon Lakes Ranger District. The guide served both as a public resource and as a demonstration of what a volunteer organization can publish with professional editorial support.

**The details.** Paperback, 128 pages, ISBN 978-0-9754039-1-4, published May 2005. The guide is now out of print, but used copies still circulate among booksellers — [BookFinder](https://www.bookfinder.com/search/?isbn=9780975403914&st=sr&ac=qr&mode=basic) compares whoever has one today.

An as-told-to memoir completed on an accelerated schedule, from 22 hours of recorded interviews.

*Sample content — replace via the CMS.*

The family called in March; the diagnosis meant the usual twelve-month memoir timeline would not work. The project was rebuilt around what mattered: get the voice on record first, shape it second.

**The work.** Twenty-two hours of interviews across six weeks, transcribed and drafted in parallel. The subject read and approved every chapter.

**The outcome.** A finished memoir in the subject's own cadence, delivered in ninety days — along with the recordings themselves, which the family says they treasure most.
Fifteen years of research into one family line, spanning 400 years of American history — now a book in editorial review, a research website, and an ongoing newsletter.
Cover of The Gunns in America — A 400-Year American Story Told Through One Family Line, by John Paul Lumpp

*The Gunns in America* began the way most family history does: with questions. Fifteen years of research later, it has become a demonstration of everything WriteNow.Media offers — research, writing, and publishing, carried through from records to readers.

**The research.** Tracing one family line across 400 years of American history meant working through the standard instruments of the trade — census schedules, vital records, land and military files, newspapers — and resolving the conflicts between them. The research continues; family history is never truly finished.

**The writing.** The findings became *The Gunns in America: A 400-Year American Story Told Through One Family Line* — a book-length narrative now in editorial review, turning the documented record into story: names into people, dates into events.

**The publishing.** The project already lives in two public forms: a research website at [gunnsinamerica.org](https://gunnsinamerica.org), and an ongoing newsletter at [gunnsinamerica.substack.com](https://gunnsinamerica.substack.com), where new finds and stories are published as the research develops.

For a client, this is what the History & Genealogy service produces: verified research, a story worth reading, and — if you want it — a way to keep the story alive.
A three-part white paper series that became the company's most-used sales asset within a quarter.

*Sample content — replace via the CMS.*

The client's product was genuinely good and genuinely hard to explain. Their existing materials were written by engineers, for engineers — and read by procurement officers who understood none of it.

**The brief.** Three white papers a non-technical buyer could read on a train.

**The work.** Interviews with engineering, sales, and — critically — three customers. The papers were structured around the buyer's questions rather than the product's architecture.

**The outcome.** Sales reports the series is now attached to every serious proposal. The company has commissioned a second series.
A 180-page documented family history, traced from an 1887 ship manifest to the present, printed for a 60-person reunion.

*This is sample content — replace with a real project via the CMS at `/keystatic`.*

The client knew one fact: a great-grandfather had "worked at the mill." Eighteen months of research produced the rest — an 1887 arrival at Ellis Island, a paper trail through three states, and a strike in 1912 that explained a family silence no one had understood.

**The brief.** Trace the paternal line as far as records allowed, and produce something the family would actually read.

**The work.** Census schedules, naturalization files, parish registers in two countries, and newspaper archives. Every fact in the finished book carries a citation; every dead end is documented honestly.

**The outcome.** A bound, illustrated narrative history delivered ahead of the family reunion — plus the complete research file, so the next generation can pick up where it left off.
What a Source List Is For
Every project here ships with its research file. Here's why that isn't just professional hygiene.

*Sample post — replace via the CMS.*

Every WriteNow project is delivered with its source list — the interviews, records, and documents behind the finished piece. Clients sometimes ask why.

Three reasons. First, verification: a claim you can trace is a claim you can defend, whether to a skeptical uncle at a reunion or a legal review at a company. Second, continuity: the next writer, or the next generation, can pick up the trail without starting over. Third, honesty: a source list shows where the record is thin, and thin spots marked honestly are worth more than confident prose over a void.

A finished piece is the visible part of the work. The file is the foundation — and it belongs to you.
